topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope landed on my desk with a soft, almost insulting thud. Another one. I picked it up with two fingers, as if handling something contaminated. The embossed lettering read: "Class of 2005 Reunion." My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I stared at the elegant script, a familiar wave of weary resignation washing over me. I hadn't even thought about those years in years, and now, this unsolicited summons arrived. I crumpled the paper once, then smoothed it out again, the crisp edges a sharp contrast to the dull throb behind my eyes. Why did everyone always have to involve me in these obligatory gatherings? I tossed it onto the pile of unopened mail, the sound barely audible in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, running a hand through my already disheveled hair. The invitation sat squarely in the center of the coffee table, mocking me with its glossy finish. I picked up my phone, intending to delete the message immediately, but my thumb hovered over the screen. The sheer volume of expected small talk, the forced smiles, the obligatory reminiscing—it all made my shoulders tense up. I let out a slow, controlled breath, the air tasting stale. I looked around the room, suddenly feeling too exposed, too aware of the impending social performance. This felt like an unnecessary imposition on my limited free time.

[story 3]

When I finally tore open the thick cardstock, the ink seemed to mock me with its cheerful, faded colors. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat. I scanned the details—the date, the venue, the mandatory RSVP deadline. Every detail felt like another hurdle to clear. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly across my chest, a physical manifestation of my displeasure. "Of course," I thought, the word sharp and brittle in my mind. "Another obligation. Just what I needed." I didn't bother to look at the contact information; I just wanted the thing to be gone.
